content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I tested 196 AI-generated apps against one Level A accessibility rule.
Can users stop the motion? 66.3% failed. Here’s how to check (and fix) yours in five minutes.
(Disclosure up front: I build MotionSpec, a tool for exactly the problem this article measures. So don’t take my word for any of it — the method is described in full, every number is reproducible from published rules, and the raw dataset is linked at the end. I’m also not naming which AI builder is which; they appear only as cohorts A–E. The point is a category pattern, not a scoreboard.)
Sonia gets motion sick from screens. Not metaphorically — parallax heroes, auto-scrolling logos and looping gradients give her the same vertigo a boat does.
So she did what the accessibility world spent a decade building for her: she turned on Reduce Motion, the switch that lives in every major operating system. It tells websites, in a language they can read, please calm down.
The web has a standard way to honour that request. The question I wanted a real number for is: in the new generation of apps that AI builders spin up from a prompt, how many actually listen?
I scanned 196 of them. Mostly, they don’t.
The numbers
I took 196 publicly accessible production apps built with five major AI app-builder platforms and ran one focused check against the two oldest, most automatable motion-accessibility rules on the books: is there a reduced-motion guard, and can you pause a loop that never stops?

• 66.3% run at least one infinite animation with no way to pause it. That’s a failure of WCAG 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide) — a Level A criterion, the floor everyone is meant to clear, not an edge case.

• 96.9% ship some motion with no reduced-motion guard. I want to be careful here, because this is the number that gets overstated: a missing guard is a best-practice gap, not a strict Level A/AA failure. It’s the ambient problem. The 66.3% is the hard one.

• 3.1% — six apps out of 196 — came back clean.

• The median app carried 17 distinct motion root causes. Not 17 CSS lines; 17 separate causes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

196 production apps from five AI builder platforms, anonymized as cohorts A–E. One platform’s defaults already move the numbers — the gap is a default, not a technical ceiling.Four of the five cohorts hit 100% on the unguarded-motion check.
That near-uniformity is the tell — this isn’t a handful of careless builders, it’s a default.
But one cohort, E, breaks the pattern: 85% instead of 100%, a quarter of the loop-failure rate of its peers, and the only cohort shipping clean apps at all. That’s the most hopeful number in the study, because it means the gap is not a technical ceiling. One platform’s defaults already move the numbers. If one can, all of them can.
Why you’ve never seen this in an accessibility report
Here’s the uncomfortable part: most of these apps would pass the accessibility scan a careful team actually runs. Not because the scanners are bad — because they don’t look at motion.
The most authoritative snapshot of the field, the WebAIM Million, analyses the top one million home pages every year. Its 2026 edition found detectable WCAG failures on 95.9% of them and flagged the first accessibility regression in seven years, tying part of it to the rise of AI-assisted “vibe coding.”
But look at what it measures: contrast, alt text, empty links and buttons, form labels, document language. Motion isn’t on the list — not because it’s rare, but because the automated scanners behind reports like that don’t reliably evaluate animation. Visual-regression tools go the other way and freeze animation on purpose so they can diff screenshots.
So let me be honest about the ranking, because the accessibility community will be, rightly: motion is not the biggest accessibility problem. Contrast and alt text are the epidemic — fix those first. Motion is a different kind of problem: the one the standard tooling can’t see, which is exactly why it keeps shipping on sites that otherwise pass. Not the largest gap. The invisible one. And an AI builder that was never asked about reduced motion will reproduce it a thousand times without a single warning.
Why this stopped being a niche footnote in 2026
Three things converged this year.
Motion became everyone’s job. shipped Figma Motion at Config 2026 — a native timeline plus an AI agent that generates animation from a prompt, on every plan. Figma’s own recap adds the caveat in the same breath: motion in the tool “does not replace reduced-motion preferences or contrast checks.” More motion, made by more people, faster, with no enforcement in the tool that makes it.
AI is generating the unsafe version at scale.
That’s what the 196 apps show. The default output of a pipeline that was never prompted about accessibility is motion with no brake.
And the law started to bite — stated carefully.
The European Accessibility Act has been in force since June 2025.
On 4 June 2026 a French court, in the Carrefour case, issued what’s regarded as the first EAA enforcement decision: an order to fix within six months or pay €500 a day.
To be precise, because this gets exaggerated online: that’s a court order with a daily penalty, not one of the “€900k–€1M fines” that circulate — those are statutory maximums nobody has actually been made to pay. The enforcement is real; the million-euro headline isn’t yet.
Whose job is this?
It would be easy, from where I sit, to point at the builders. I won’t — partly because it isn’t fair, and partly because it isn’t true. The people using these tools aren’t careless, and the tools aren’t malicious. The gap exists because the whole chain optimised for shipping something that looks alive, and nobody in it was ever asked to also let it stop.
That includes me. MotionSpec shipped unguarded motion on its own pages before we started measuring — which is, honestly, the only reason I know where to look. Writers like (who argues for a “no-motion-first” default) and (whose work on motion sensitivity predates the tools now making it worse) have been clear about who this affects for years. The point of a study isn’t to relitigate the should. It’s to measure the is — and to make the gap legible to the people now generating motion who never read those posts.
The fix is genuinely small
None of this is exotic. Two patterns cover most of what I found.
First, honour the reduced-motion request:
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
*, *::before, *::after {
animation-duration: .01ms !important;
animation-iteration-count: 1 !important;
transition-duration: .01ms !important;
}
}That’s a blunt safety net — a real implementation guards specific animations rather than blanket-killing them — but even the net beats nothing. Second, give anything that loops forever an off switch: a pause control wired to animation-play-state, or simply a finite animation-iteration-count. That single change is what moves an app out of the 66.3%.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Before/after panels of the same page. Before: still animating — ignores the reduce-motion setting. After: calm — respects the setting.The guard, live: the same page with reduced-motion honoured and ignored (try the toggle yourself). In an article about motion nobody can stop, that’s not a limitation — it’s the whole argument.
The 5-minute Claude check
The title promised five minutes, so here is the clock. (Same disclosure as the top: both tools below are mine. Both are free, keyless, and store nothing you submit.)
Minutes 0–2 — scan your UI.
Paste your URL into the free motion check. It runs the same static scan as this study — linked CSS and style blocks against WCAG 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide) and 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions) — and returns every finding with its fix. If it flags an infinite loop with no pause path, you’re in the 66.3%.
Minutes 2–4 — hand the findings to Claude.
The two patterns above are ordinary CSS; paste the findings into Claude and ask for the guarded version. To keep the fix from regressing the next time an AI touches your UI, connect the MotionSpec MCP server — one URL, https://api.motionspec.dev/mcp, no API key. From then on Claude proposes motion only from a catalog of 40 human-reviewed primitives, each with a reduced-motion fallback built in, and motion_validate fails closed: animation that isn't on the reviewed allow-list doesn't ship. Two-minute setup guide, or try motion_validate in the browser playground first.
Here’s the question I’d be asking if I ran one of these builder platforms.
Today, the measured saving is modest — honest numbers first: emitting a small spec against an allow-list instead of regenerating animation code runs about 1.3–2× cheaper on the same model. Real, but not headline material.
The compounding part is structural. A spec is deterministic. Deterministic means cacheable.
Cacheable means the same motion never has to be generated twice.
So as the reviewed catalog grows and repeat coverage climbs, the modeled ceiling sits around 10–25×. Modeled — not promised.
But the direction only points one way: every primitive added makes the next thousand generations cheaper.
And that’s where this stops being a cost story.
The first AI builder that ships verified motion as a default isn’t just safer than its competitors — it gets structurally cheaper and faster with every app it generates.
That’s not an advantage rivals can out-market. They can only copy it.
Which is how defaults change.
Cohort E suggests someone is already partway there. The measurements and the mechanisms: why a motion spec cuts token costs and hallucinations.
Minute 5 — re-run the scan. Clean means your loops pause and your motion respects the reduce-motion switch — Sonia’s vertigo settings finally get an answer. The business version of that sentence: people who get dizzy on your site don’t file a bug report. They close the tab.
“The models hallucinated the motion; the humans got the vertigo. Fix the first, spare the second.”
Method, in brief
This is a static scan of each page’s linked CSS and inline styles — nothing else, which makes every number a lower bound: runtime JavaScript motion (GSAP, the Web Animations API, scroll libraries) isn’t measured, and only each app’s public entry page was visited. Corpus: 196 apps, 21–45 per cohort, collected July 16, 2026 with recorded provenance, robots.txt respected, login-walled apps excluded. Reduced-motion guards map to WCAG 2.3.3 territory; pause paths for infinite motion map to WCAG 2.2.2 (Level A). Neither is fully machine-checkable — "essential" motion is a human judgment — so these are automatable failure patterns, not a conformance verdict or a legal determination for any app or company.
The full write-up, per-cohort breakdown, and the anonymized dataset (CC-BY-4.0, no URLs, no platform names) live on the original: State of Motion in AI-Generated UIs.
Every AI learned to make the web move. Almost none learned to let it stop. That’s fixable — it starts with one media query.
